segments.text: Then I'll pass over to Kate.
So, um, Miranda Koto.
My name's Jason Hewitt, manager of Performance Audits.
Um, joined here today, um, by Kate Williams,
the audit lead for this piece of work.
Um, and, uh, clin, uh,
audit director from Audit New Zealand.
So I'll, that's, that's my part
so far. So I'll pass you on to Kate.
Thanks, Jason. Yeah, so I'm just gonna talk a bit about,
um, why we did this audit, what we found,
and then we can move on to any questions that you've got.
Sure. Um, we did this audit
because vehicle safety is important.
Um, if you've got a car, you'll know about getting a warrant
of fitness, but even if you don't have a car, you know,
you use our roads, you might go on the bus, you might bike.
So you wanna know that the vehicles on our roads are safe.
We looked at this audit, or for this audit, we looked at
how well, um, NZTA regulates the vehicle inspectors
and inspecting organizations that carry out warrants
and certificates of fitness.
We looked at how they, how they appoint inspectors.
We looked at how they detect and respond to non-compliance.
So when they're not doing the job properly,
and we looked at how they, um, monitor
and report on the work that they do.
Back in 2018,
a car passenger died when their seatbelt
failed in an accident.
The, um, the car had been recently checked
and given a warrant of fitness,
but it turned out that, um,
the car had not been inspected properly.
As a result of this. NZTA carried out some, um,
investigations and found that they had
what they called a regulatory failure in terms
of the warrant and certificates of fitness.
They found there were, I think about 850 cases
of non-compliance that they hadn't followed up,
and about 150 of those, they deemed to be,
um, high priority.
So after that, there were some reviews, a lot
of changes were made, and,
and we came in, um, some years after that to do the audit.
What we found was that NCTA now has much better processes in
place to detect non-compliance and to respond quickly
and consistently when they find non-compliance.
And that was a, you know, a very good finding.
But we did find some areas that they also need to improve.
So one of those is around it.
Um, the regulation and warrants
and certificates of fitness still uses a lot of quite, um,
outdated IT systems.
So the main database that they use, um, for the,
for the inspections, there's an old access database.
A lot of their work is based on the spreadsheets
for tracking their work and things like that.
We also found that the vehicle standards themselves could be
easier to find and understand,
and we thought that there could be clearer links
between the work or,
or the impact of warrant certificates of fitness
and the impact that has on overall road safety,
because without those links, it makes it hard to see
what the impact of that work is.
We also talked a bit about the future.
So with a warrant or certificate of fitness at the moment,
they take a vehicle and they do pretty
much a physical check of it.
You know, they sound the horn, they test the brakes,
they have a look to see if there's any
rust on the undercarriage.
But more and more cars now have got very modern, um,
safety features, which are based on advanced technology.
And those things aren't looked at in a
warrant or certificate of fitness.
And at the moment, you know,
nobody's really looking at that.
So that's something that needs
to be dealt with in the future.
NZTA and the Ministry of Transport are aware of that,
and there is work starting, they're starting to think about,
um, what that might look like
and how we might have assurance about
vehicle safety in the future.
Just for the committee, um,
there's a few things the committee might think about.
As a result of this audit, we made five recommendations
and our report, the committee might like to ask NZTA, um,
to come in and talk about their progress, um,
against those recommendations
for the work being done about the future
of vehicle safety testing.
The Ministry of Transport might be better
placed to talk about that work.
Um, also some of our findings might apply
to other regulators like maritime, um, maritime New Zealand.
Um, there might be questions for those regulators such as
how are they using data
and analytics to support their work, um,
whether they have got clear outcomes
and can see what the outcomes are from their regulatory work
and you know, what they're doing about looking to the future
to see how things might need to change
as technology changes.
So thank you. We're happy
to answer any questions that you've got. Thanks, Kate.
Um, Dan, you had fir you were first up.
Oh yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, really good.
Interesting report. Uh, thank you.
Um, was this, I'm just quite kinda understand in terms
of why you chose this area.
Was it, um, did you have inquiries in your office to,
to look at this or,
Um, there wasn't any particular trigger?
Um, yeah, it was just an area that we thought was,
was important, something that we hadn't looked at before.
Yeah. Fantastic. The other thing is, um,
I heard you talk about technology
and definitely like modern cars these days.
Mm-hmm. Just more like, um, modern lap laptops on wheels.
So, um, you know, I know my two vehicles have a lot of tech
that just regulates even the,
the handbrake now is electronic rather than physical.
Mm-hmm. So how do, how do inspectors build that into
their regime?
Um, you know that we are moving
to basically electronics controlling most
of Yeah. The car system.
So there's, there's a couple of things there.
So if it's a, um, an electronic thing
that's replacing an existing feature,
then I think they would test it
to some extent if it functions in the same way.
So one of the examples that they talked to us about was
how some cars, um,
now rather than having mirrors, they have cameras.
And this was a bit of a problem to start with
because the regulations were very rigid
and said they needed to have a mirror.
And they've now had to change that to say they need
to have a mirror or something that does the same job
as a mirror that can see behind the car.
So they can change the regulations to some extent,
but they don't go as far
as testing the actual hardware or software of it.
They just would check that you can see behind the vehicle.
But where there are, are new features like, you know,
lane keep assist or something like that, that is not checked
as part of a, a warrant of fitness. Mm.
Which would be concerned given sooner
or later those might start having faults and
Yeah. And there's more and
more vehicles have them,
it's gonna be more and more of a, of an issue.
Yes. And so that's why, you know, the Ministry
of Transport is already looking into that.
And in terms of legislative change, I mean,
I guess we've got clunky old, older legislation of,
did your organization come up
with any recommendations on the legislative front or,
I think the main recommendation was
around just really making, um, it easier
to change the leg legislation.
Mm-hmm. So, you know, um, even a quite a minor change,
because the regulations are secondary legislation,
there is a process to go through
and so NZTA can't necessarily just make a change
that makes sense easily without going
through a full legislative process for
that secondary legislation.
Yeah, correct. Thank you. So can I just supplement you?
I'm sorry. So is that, um,
so you reckon the current checklist, you know,
when you do well, it's not fit for purpose anymore.
Like there's certain things need
to be add on to be checked as well.
I don't know whether say it's fit for purpose
or not, it's not really our role to say, say
what our inspection should cover.
I think we are just saying that it doesn't cover those
things, and that's a challenge that needs to be considered,
um, by the policy makers to think about, well, what,
what is the purpose of these inspections?
What do they need to cover? And then
how, how do you address that?
Um, I think, you know, the way that, um,
inspections are done at the moment
and the workforce that does inspections at the moment
is not necessarily the right, right.
Workforce with the right skillset to go
and start testing advanced technologies,
a whole different approach might be needed.
So it's quite a major piece of work that's going to need
to be done to look at those advanced technologies
and how we get that assurance,
and then also who's responsible for, for that.
Sure. Thank you. Can I, Camilla,
You go first, Um, just a recommendation five.
Um, and I understand about technologies changing,
but you've kind of recommended there that, um,
that the inspection requirements are easier to change.
Do you think that that's too permissive in terms of, uh,
relying on technology changes being sufficient

uh, as, as people have said, they might have faults

Um, and do you think change is the right word,
or do you think modify
Is it might maybe modify?
Yeah, so we are not really talking about the major changes.
Um, so at the moment there are, you know, there's a rule

and it just specifies it's quite functional, um, about
what it needs, what it needs to do,
what they need to be able to do.
Um, but sometimes maybe that doesn't work
or there's a bit of a change
that means it just doesn't quite make sense.
And at the moment, that's quite hard to change,
but I think for a more, um, for a more substantive change,
then a whole different approach might be needed anyway.
Yeah. Thanks. Can I have
Yeah, go on. Yeah,
sure. Uh,

we didn't look at that side of it.

So do you think that

all aspects of the technology, um,
or any, anything that's relevant.

of those things because it will pick up missing data.
You know, in terms of if they're manipulating the data, then
that's where it's really down to the,
the owner if they think
that something's not being done properly. Um, yeah.
So, so just now you've mentioned this
is not a perfect system.
So from your point of view, how can we improve that system
so we can truly effect, you know,
the data is truly effect what's going on?
Is it, is there any idea or, or,
I, I think the data is probably, probably the key.
So that, as I said, they do some data analysis at the
moment, what we think they could do is expand on
that data analysis
and that would help if they had, um, improved
and updated IT systems.
But, you know, they use some various measures
that we've talked about, so pass some fail rates
as a few other ones that they use,
but they could cut that data in different ways
and perhaps dig into it in different ways to see
where there are more risks.
Um, you know, what sorts of inspectors
or inspecting organizations perhaps have a higher risk
of non-compliance and look at those in in more detail.
But really, um, you know, when you've got, as you said,
quite a small number of in inspect of people at NZTA
and a big population of vehicle inspectors
and the data's one way that it's quite efficient if you use
that data well of, um, you know,
of picking up where there are problems.
Sure. Just
In terms of the recommendations, um, this,
this report was February, um, so obviously been some, um,
potential for NCTA and MOT to respond.
So what's their, you know, when they've got the report,
what's the feedback they've been that they've given you and,
and what have they done as a response to it?
So, I mean, the response at the time was they were
supportive of the findings.
Um, we haven't talked to them about it since, since then.
Um, but we do have a standard follow up procedure,
so probably within another 12 to 18 months we would go back
to them formally 18 months as a follow up
and ask them about the progress that they've made.
And, um, we'll publish that information. Yeah.
What would you suggest that we, that we should do
with this in terms of following it up?
Because the, 'cause we will obviously go 'cause mm-hmm.
A lot of these things you said, well,
that's actually their business rather than you're Yeah.
You know, you're around their system, um, about
how the system's actually working, um,
and particularly some
of those things about the new technologies, et cetera,
as well as the, as well as the mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm. The inspecting the inspector system, um,
and responding to your recommendations.
So what, what would, what would your recommendation
to us be in terms of what we should do?
Yes. I think, as Jason said,
probably the recommendations three and
and five are probably the key ones.
And, you know, there's obviously been a lot of interest
with the committee, um, about the, you know, the challenges
of the technology and,
and changing technology and what that means.

what progress they're making with that policy work.
So they were due to start, um, well,
they'd already done some, some thinking about it,

specific questions that you've got.
Um, also the interest in data
that might be something you could follow up with NZTA, um,
talk to 'em about, you know, what they are doing.
Um, they might be able to explain in a bit more detail about

around the, any, any changes that are,

Legislation. Yeah. Okay. Cool.
Um, I'm not sure I had anything else at this stage.

No. No. Okay. But
otherwise, just say thank you very much for,
for all the work that's obviously gone into this

Yeah. And, um, thank you.
And we will, we will consider how we follow up.
